header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence
header.stance: Oil relief pulled the front end lower.

overall_summary.headline: Inflation relief stopped short of cheaper long-duration capital
overall_summary.summary: Lower oil and breakevens pulled policy-sensitive Treasury yields down while equities, semiconductors and volatility confirmed immediate risk relief. An unchanged 10Y real yield, a 30Y auction near 5.06%, and highly structured corporate and sovereign financing kept the long-horizon cost of capital elevated.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Falling crude and breakevens led a front-end Treasury rally that growth assets accepted, while the unchanged real yield and expensive 30Y clearing level limited the move along the curve.

watch_events.title: U.S. CPI
watch_events.watch_line: June CPI is the first test of the breakeven-led rally.
watch_events.market_trigger: Headline CPI versus -0.1% monthly consensus and core CPI versus 0.3%.
watch_events.market_implication: Core composition will determine whether relief reaches real yields and the long bond.
watch_events.confirms_if: Core inflation is benign and breakevens extend lower without a real-yield rebound.
watch_events.challenges_if: Core prices surprise higher and the 30Y returns above the auction clearing area.
watch_events.why_it_matters: It is the nearest top-tier test of the session's inflation relief.

watch_events.title: U.S. PPI
watch_events.watch_line: Producer prices test whether pipeline inflation agrees with CPI.
watch_events.market_trigger: Monthly PPI versus 0.2% consensus after the prior 1.1% increase.
watch_events.market_implication: Agreement would reinforce lower breakevens; divergence would preserve curve and term-premium pressure.
watch_events.confirms_if: Pipeline prices cool alongside CPI and refined-product pressure eases.
watch_events.challenges_if: PPI remains firm despite softer consumer inflation or benchmark crude.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Pipeline costs can reverse a headline-energy disinflation signal.

watch_events.title: U.S. Retail Sales
watch_events.watch_line: Retail sales follow inflation with a direct demand test.
watch_events.market_trigger: Advance sales versus 0.3% consensus after a 0.9% prior gain.
watch_events.market_implication: Demand resilience will set the real-rate response after CPI and PPI.
watch_events.confirms_if: Sales cool enough to broaden duration demand beyond inflation compensation.
watch_events.challenges_if: Strong consumption keeps policy pricing and real yields firm.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Demand determines whether lower inflation becomes broader policy relief.

watch_events.title: Korea Auction And BOK
watch_events.watch_line: A 10Y auction clears before a hawkish policy decision.
watch_events.market_trigger: July 13 auction demand followed by the July 16 BOK rate decision and guidance.
watch_events.market_implication: The sequence prices the concession needed for Asian duration ahead of tighter policy.
watch_events.confirms_if: The auction clears smoothly and policy guidance stays within the priced path.
watch_events.challenges_if: Weak demand or a stronger hike signal pushes Korean long yields and KRW pressure higher.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Korean 10Y, KRW and Asian sovereign curves
watch_events.why_it_matters: Auction absorption arrives immediately before a policy repricing test.

watch_events.title: UK GDP
watch_events.watch_line: May growth meets an unsettled long-gilt issuance plan.
watch_events.market_trigger: Monthly GDP versus 0.1% consensus after a 0.1% decline.
watch_events.market_implication: Growth and fiscal expectations will compete in the long end of the gilt curve.
watch_events.confirms_if: Moderate growth allows policy expectations to ease without worsening fiscal compensation.
watch_events.challenges_if: Weak activity raises fiscal concern or firm growth sustains tightening expectations.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Long gilts, UK curve and European duration
watch_events.why_it_matters: The release lands while debt managers reconsider long-maturity supply.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: Lower crude and softer breakevens supported duration.

cross_asset_cards.title: Equities
cross_asset_cards.summary: Semiconductor leadership showed equities could absorb the rates move.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: Treasury yields were lower, while a softer dollar and gold strength left residual hedging.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 9 close delivered an inflation-relief rally without broadly repricing the cost of capital. Crude fell more than 2%, breakevens narrowed, and policy-sensitive Treasury sectors outperformed the long bond. The curve steepened as the front end rallied, while the 10-year real yield held at 2.31% and the 30-year remained near 5%. Lower inflation compensation therefore drove the nominal move; real borrowing costs stayed high. Equities and semiconductors advanced as volatility fell, confirming that investors treated the rates move as immediate risk relief instead of a sudden growth shock. Gold also rose, preserving demand for protection against geopolitical escalation and a renewed inflation impulse.

Funding markets described a similarly constructive but expensive environment. Listed investment-grade and high-yield gauges edged higher, and ION Platform's 2032 bond rose 3.5 points after stronger earnings and lower cash distributions. At the same time, the financing calendar showed how much structure investors still required. A record $35 billion private-credit package tied to Broadcom is moving toward secondary trading, TeraWulf is preparing about $3.5 billion of leveraged debt for an AI data-center project, Barclays is distributing acquisition loans, and Shapoorji Pallonji has begun a 255 billion-rupee refinancing. SK Hynix attracted demand approaching $200 billion for a $26.5 billion equity listing, while MiniMax combined shares with zero-coupon convertible bonds. Vestel, by contrast, entered restructuring discussions on a deeply distressed $500 million bond after a downgrade to CCC-. Capital was available across public, private, secured, and hybrid channels, but borrower cash flow, collateral, maturity relief, and strategic importance still determined the price.

The same distinction appeared in sovereign duration and EM. Japan's five-year auction found broadly normal demand at a 1.905% yield, yet longer JGBs remained under pressure from fiscal and policy risk. UK debt managers had not sold bonds maturing in 15 years or more three months into the fiscal year, despite £22.4 billion of planned long-dated issuance. A slightly softer dollar and a 0.18% gain in EMB improved the common EM backdrop, but Indonesia's carry trade, China's local-debt cleanup, Senegal's IMF path, and Egypt's cautious policy stance remained separate country stories. The next test is much closer than a second-half inflation theme: U.S. CPI arrives July 14, PPI July 15, and retail sales July 16. Those releases will determine whether the session's fall in breakevens can extend into real yields and long duration, or whether July 9 remains a front-end relief move around an expensive long bond.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasuries rallied along a clear maturity gradient. The 2-year yield fell 5 basis points to 4.16%, the 5-year fell 4 basis points to 4.27%, the 10-year declined 2 basis points to 4.54%, and the 30-year eased just 1 basis point to 5.05%. The result was a bull-steepening curve: 2s10s widened 3 basis points to 38 basis points and 2s30s widened 4 basis points to 89 basis points. The front end and belly responded to a friendlier near-term inflation mix, while the long bond retained most of its term-premium and supply compensation. The curve therefore carried two messages at once. The market was more comfortable with the immediate policy horizon, but it had not reduced the return demanded for holding duration through fiscal borrowing, inflation uncertainty, and a large global capital-spending cycle.

The real-yield and breakeven split identified the source of the rally. The 10-year breakeven fell 2 basis points to 2.23%, while the 10-year TIPS yield was unchanged at 2.31%. Crude supplied the cleanest same-day channel: WTI dropped to $71.86 and Brent to $76.10, both down more than 2%. Lower benchmark energy prices reduced the probability of a renewed near-term inflation impulse and allowed nominal yields to fall. Real borrowing costs did not follow. With the real yield still above 2.3%, the hurdle rate for housing, infrastructure, leveraged acquisitions, and long-lived technology investment remained high. The session improved the inflation component of the nominal curve without changing the price of real duration.

The economic data did not create a labor-led easing story. Initial jobless claims were 215,000, below the 220,000 consensus, and continuing claims were 1.814 million, also slightly below expectations. Fewer claims preserved the picture of a resilient labor market. Housing supplied more evidence of rate restraint: existing-home sales fell 2.4% to a 4.09 million annual pace, mortgage rates remained near 6.6%, and the median price still rose 1.8% from a year earlier to a record $440,600. Turnover was weak, but limited affordability and firm prices pointed to a constrained market instead of an abrupt collapse in demand. That combination helps explain why the Treasury move concentrated in inflation compensation. Activity is feeling the level of rates, yet labor and nominal prices have not weakened enough to force a broad policy reversal.

The 30-year reopening placed a market price on that distinction. The $22 billion sale stopped around 5.061%, the highest auction yield in nearly two decades, with the bond trading near that level into the close. Demand existed and the auction cleared, but buyers required a yield above 5% to absorb long supply. The result did not turn into a disorderly selloff, which allowed the front-end rally to survive. It also offered no evidence that the market's long-run concerns had disappeared. Government borrowing, corporate AI infrastructure spending, and global sovereign issuance all compete for long-duration balance sheet. A clearing yield near 5.06% says that capacity is available at a high price.

Fed communication kept that long-run question open. New York Fed President John Williams identified AI-driven demand as a potential inflation source that policy could not automatically look through when it pushed demand persistently above supply. That channel links the rates and credit stories: the same investment boom that creates large private-credit, leveraged-loan, convertible, and equity transactions can also sustain demand for labor, power, equipment, and financing. Planned revisions to the PCE methodology may lower measured core inflation by an estimated 0.1 to 0.3 percentage point compared with the current approach. The measurement change is relevant to the policy debate, but it is not the closest market event. July 14 CPI and July 15 PPI arrive first, followed by retail sales on July 16. Their composition will determine whether the July 9 rally was an early disinflation signal or simply a one-day energy adjustment.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit tone improved at the margin, with an important limit on the claim. LQD gained 0.04%, HYG rose 0.11%, and JNK added 0.10%. Those listed gauges showed that the Treasury rally and stronger equities did not destabilize fixed-income risk assets, but their small moves did not establish a broad tightening in cash spreads. ION Platform provided a more direct issuer-level signal. Its €600 million bond due in 2032 rose 3.5 points to 78.6 after second-quarter revenue increased 5.1%, EBITDA climbed 14.3%, and cash distributions to the parent fell to $53 million from $314 million in the prior quarter. Investors rewarded better operating performance and less leakage from a leveraged capital structure. That was a specific credit improvement, not a market-wide verdict.

The largest financing stories centered on AI infrastructure, but they reached investors through very different instruments. A portion of the $35 billion Broadcom-linked private-credit package arranged by Apollo is expected to become tradable over the coming months, with about $15 billion potentially available by early 2027. Investors expect roughly $24 billion of the package to be drawn by summer 2027, and Broadcom backstops payments on the largest senior pieces. The planned secondary market would create more price discovery for the largest private-credit transaction on record. It would also move concentrated exposure from an origination structure into portfolios that must assess liquidity, collateral, tenant credit, and long-dated technology demand.

TeraWulf is preparing another version of the same capital requirement. The company is considering about $3.5 billion of leveraged loans and high-yield bonds for a Kentucky data-center campus after signing a 20-year lease with Anthropic that is expected to generate about $19 billion of revenue. The long contract supplies a cash-flow case for lenders, while the leveraged-loan and junk-bond structure transfers construction, execution, and technology-cycle risk to credit investors. Williams's comments on AI demand show the macro side of the transaction. Funding can remain available for projects with strategic demand and contracted revenue even while the investment wave keeps real rates and the inflation reaction function firm.

Equity and hybrid markets absorbed strategic technology supply as well. SK Hynix priced its U.S. listing at $149 per ADR, raising about $26.5 billion after orders approached $200 billion. The deal became the largest first-time U.S. share sale by a foreign company, and its depth matched the 3.1% rise in the semiconductor index. MiniMax pursued a more layered structure, marketing up to HK$14.54 billion of new shares and convertible bonds. The package included HK$6.5 billion of zero-coupon converts due in 2027 with a 25% conversion premium. A zero coupon lowers immediate cash interest, while the conversion option gives investors equity upside and leaves dilution as part of the issuer's capital cost. These transactions showed strong demand for AI-linked exposure, but they did not imply that straight unsecured debt had become uniformly cheap.

Conventional acquisition and refinancing markets also remained open. Barclays was preparing to distribute about $875 million of debt used for the takeover of Senior, including $685 million and $190 million senior-secured term-loan facilities alongside a $150 million revolver. The structure shifts underwritten risk from bank balance sheets to institutional lenders while preserving senior security and liquidity support. In India, Shapoorji Pallonji began a 255 billion-rupee refinancing with about 150 billion rupees of local bonds. Farallon, Davidson Kempner, and Cerberus were among the expected investors, and Deutsche Bank could commit about $400 million. The proceeds address debt that had already received two maturity extensions. Large pools of capital were willing to refinance the group, but only after a bespoke solution assembled private investors, local bonds, and a substantial arranging-bank position.

The boundary of that access was visible at Vestel. The Turkish electronics maker hired Houlihan Lokey for talks on its $500 million bond due in May 2029 while renegotiating local bank loans. Fitch had cut the issuer two notches to CCC- and described the capital structure as unsustainable after first-quarter revenue fell by almost half. Strong equity markets and a lower Treasury front end did not repair that borrower profile. The relevant variables were operating deterioration, debt service, and the terms bondholders and banks would accept in a restructuring.

The credit conclusion is therefore narrower and more useful than a broad risk-on label. Financing channels are functioning for strategic projects, acquisitions, maturity takeouts, and issuers with improving cash generation. The market is distributing risk through private credit, secured loans, local bonds, equity, and convertibles according to the cash flows and protections available. New-issue books, final pricing, retained bank exposure, and secondary trading will show whether July 9's better market tone lowers the cost of capital. Announcement volume alone cannot answer that question.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Japan showed how higher yields can restore demand in one part of a curve without resolving pressure elsewhere. The five-year JGB auction cleared at 1.905% with demand broadly in line with recent sales. Elevated yields attracted buyers, yet bond futures softened after the result and longer maturities remained under pressure from fiscal spending plans and concern that the BOJ was moving too slowly against inflation. A former BOJ official estimated that the policy rate could eventually reach 2% or slightly more, above the 1.75% terminal-rate consensus, after the latest increase took the benchmark to 1%. The auction demonstrated intermediate-sector absorption. The policy and fiscal debate continued to raise the price of long Japanese duration.

Korea and Malaysia illustrated the cost of averaging regional policy signals. Korean guidance argued for another rate increase when inflation, stronger growth, the exchange rate, and financial imbalances aligned. Lower oil offered some relief, but domestic demand limited the disinflation benefit. Malaysia held at 2.75%, as expected, with a firmer ringgit cushioning imported inflation and giving Bank Negara more room to wait. One central bank was preparing markets for tighter policy while another could hold because currency performance improved its inflation trade-off. Asian duration remained a country-level policy market, not a synchronized easing trade.

Europe produced another form of partial relief. Germany's two-year yield fell 2 basis points to 2.68% after jumping 12 basis points the previous day as oil markets calmed. Swaps still assigned about a 90% probability to a September ECB increase. The retracement therefore stabilized the curve without undoing the policy repricing. Energy prices remained part of the inflation channel, but traders were already separating short-lived oil moves from the broader case for tighter policy.

Fiscal transmission was clearest in the UK. Investors questioned whether the government would complete £22.4 billion of planned long-dated issuance. Three months into the fiscal year, the Debt Management Office had not sold bonds maturing in 15 years or more. The absence of supply at the long end did not remove the borrowing requirement; it signaled that maturity design may have to adjust to demand. Issuing shorter debt can reduce the immediate duration concession, but it raises rollover exposure and leaves the government more sensitive to future policy rates. The same buyer discipline that held the U.S. 30-year near 5% was shaping debt-management choices abroad.

Europe's common-debt debate carried a longer-horizon version of the issue. Germany and France questioned a Spanish proposal to expand EU-wide issuance even as Christine Lagarde argued that Europe needed a common asset able to compete with Treasuries. One proposal envisioned a pool of roughly €5 trillion within five years and potential interest savings of €25 billion annually. A larger common market could improve liquidity and benchmark status, but it also requires political agreement over issuance and fiscal responsibility. Global duration supply is therefore growing through national and potential supranational channels at the same time that buyers are demanding more compensation for long maturities.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

The common EM backdrop improved modestly. DXY slipped 0.13% to 100.918 and EMB gained 0.18%. The combination eased a small amount of dollar pressure and showed that listed hard-currency debt participated in the broader risk recovery. The move was too small to overwhelm country fundamentals. Energy exposure, local inflation, refinancing calendars, political agreements, and IMF access continued to decide where lower global pressure could translate into durable demand.

Indonesia was the clearest carry story. Foreign inflows into rupiah sovereign bonds reached a 13-month high in June after policymakers used higher yields to stabilize a currency damaged by the Iran conflict. The benchmark 10-year yield remained high enough to attract global buyers, while the retreat in oil reduced fears of imported inflation and another immediate Bank Indonesia increase. The next stage depends on persistence. Some investors identified crude near $65-$70 for roughly two months and normalized traffic through the Strait of Hormuz as conditions for adding more Indonesia and India duration. July 9 moved oil in that direction, but one session did not yet deliver the required improvement in external balances.

China's story combined weak consumer inflation with a more difficult debt-management transition. June CPI rose 1.2% from a year earlier, below the 1.3% consensus, while PPI increased 3.9%, above the 3.5% expectation. The consumer side preserved room for policy support, while factory prices made the inflation picture less uniform. At the same time, authorities discouraged bankers from underwriting new LGFV bonds shorter than two years. Weaker municipal borrowers have relied on short maturities because investors doubt their long-run repayment capacity. Restricting that channel can improve debt discipline, but it also forces refinancing risk into longer structures or other official solutions.

The hidden-debt swap program showed the scale of the official balance-sheet response. Issuance had reached about 94% of a 6 trillion-yuan allowance by the end of June, after regions raised more than 1.62 trillion yuan in the first half. Moving hidden liabilities into explicit local-government bonds reduces opacity and can lower near-term funding pressure. It also uses fiscal capacity that might otherwise support demand. China therefore has policy room, but the form of support is constrained by an already large debt-cleanup program. That is more immediate to local fixed-income markets than a generic expectation of later stimulus.

Senegal and Egypt showed two different IMF transmission channels. Senegal's political rupture raised the probability of a debt reprofiling, which could extend or defer payments without the broader principal reduction associated with a restructuring. Heavy repayments run through 2028, and a new IMF program is central to restoring confidence after hidden liabilities suspended the prior arrangement. Political agreement over debt treatment is now part of the market-access condition. Egypt held its deposit and lending rates at 19% and 20% after inflation slowed for a third month to 14.3%. A preliminary IMF review could unlock more than $1.6 billion, but renewed geopolitical pressure kept the central bank cautious. Program financing and disinflation improved the position, while high nominal rates still reflected the cost of maintaining credibility.

Thailand and Peru added fiscal and inflation boundaries. Thailand's court cleared a 400 billion-baht, or roughly $11.95 billion, emergency borrowing decree for stimulus and energy-transition programs. Legal clearance allows issuance and spending to proceed, while the 70%-of-GDP debt ceiling remains the fiscal constraint investors will monitor. Peru held its policy rate at 4.25% for a tenth meeting even though annual inflation reached 4.01%, above the target band. Policymakers expect the rise to fade, but El Niño creates a food-price risk through crop yields. Both countries had room to maintain their chosen policy path, but that room came from local legal, fiscal, and inflation judgments rather than the slight decline in DXY.

## Cross asset

Risk assets confirmed that the Treasury rally was compatible with growth. The S&P 500 rose 0.8%, the Nasdaq 100 gained 1.6%, and the semiconductor index advanced 3.1%, while the VIX fell 6.3% to 15.84. That composition separated July 9 from a defensive duration rally built on sudden recession fear. Technology and semiconductor demand remained strong enough to support equity prices and exceptionally large capital raises, even as lower nominal yields improved discount-rate conditions.

Energy sent a more complicated inflation signal. WTI fell 2.3% to $71.86 and Brent declined 2.5% to $76.10, helping the 10-year breakeven move lower. Refined-product economics remained much tighter than benchmark crude suggested. The gasoline crack spread was near $57 a barrel, close to its highest level since June 2022, and diesel margins were also elevated relative to prewar levels. Physical supply was fragile as energy traffic through the Strait of Hormuz came close to a halt and Qatar paused work to restore production at a major LNG facility. Lower crude can ease market inflation expectations quickly, while refining capacity, shipping, and product shortages reach household prices with a lag. The distinction helps explain the nominal rally and the unchanged real yield.

The remaining cross-asset rows showed marginally easier financial conditions with continued hedge demand. DXY slipped to 100.918, listed credit and EMB edged higher, and the equity rally broadened beyond the S&P 500. Gold rose 1.5% to $4,133.30 despite stronger risk assets and lower volatility. Investors were willing to own growth and credit exposure while retaining protection against geopolitical escalation and inflation. The close was constructive across the immediate market board, but it did not establish that the physical energy shock or the real cost of capital had been removed.

## Forward Market Significance

U.S. CPI on July 14 is the first and most urgent test. Consensus calls for a 0.1% monthly decline in headline CPI and a 0.3% increase in core prices. A soft headline reading driven by energy would support the breakeven move already visible on July 9. The more important rates response will come from core composition and real yields. A benign core print alongside another decline in breakevens could pull the 5-year and 10-year sectors further into the rally. A firmer core reading would expose how little real yields changed and could return the 30-year to the auction's 5.061% clearing area or higher.

PPI follows on July 15, with consensus at 0.2% month on month after a 1.1% prior increase. The sequence matters more than either release in isolation. CPI will show the consumer-price result, while PPI will indicate whether pipeline costs support or challenge it. Elevated gasoline and diesel margins make that distinction useful. A soft CPI print can coexist with producer or product-price pressure that reaches households later. Agreement between the two releases would strengthen the inflation-relief interpretation; divergence would keep curve steepening and term-premium risk in place.

Advance retail sales on July 16 will test the demand side, with consensus for a 0.3% increase after 0.9% previously. July 9 housing data already showed that high financing costs are suppressing transactions without producing broad price weakness. Retail sales will reveal whether household demand remains firm enough to preserve the real-rate hurdle after the inflation releases. Strong demand following benign CPI and PPI could leave the front end supported while limiting the long end. Weaker sales with softer inflation would provide a broader case for duration than the oil-led move alone.

Global duration has two dated secondary tests. Korea sells 10-year debt on July 13 before the BOK decision on July 16. Auction demand will show the yield concession required immediately ahead of a hawkish policy meeting, and the BOK's language will determine whether that clearing level was sufficient. UK May GDP also arrives July 16, with 0.1% monthly growth expected after a 0.1% decline. The print will meet a gilt market already debating how much long-dated issuance investors will absorb. Weak growth can lower policy expectations, but a deterioration in the fiscal outlook can keep long yields elevated.

Food and energy complete the near-term inflation calendar. The July 13 WASDE report can alter agricultural supply expectations, while crude persistence, refined-product margins, and Hormuz traffic will determine whether benchmark oil relief reaches consumer prices and EM external balances. Traffic was already close to a halt and Qatar had paused work to restore major LNG production, leaving a wide gap between lower futures and physical supply conditions. Indonesia's bond case becomes more durable with crude around $65-$70 for an extended period and normalized shipping. Renewed pressure in physical flows would raise import costs, support breakevens, and narrow the room for Asian central banks to ease.

The next week therefore has a clear order. CPI sets the first inflation response, PPI tests the pipeline, and retail sales shows whether demand is cooling enough to reduce real-rate pressure. Korea and the UK supply the global-duration read-through, while oil and shipping determine whether the inflation relief survives outside financial markets. Those dated releases and observable prices are the immediate agenda. PCE methodology changes and later policy meetings belong behind them until they enter the tradable horizon.

section_summaries.summary: Lower oil and breakevens drove a front-end-led Treasury rally that equities and volatility accepted, while the unchanged 10Y real yield and a 30Y auction near 5.06% kept long-duration funding expensive. Credit issuance remained active across AI, acquisition and refinancing structures; global curves and EM sovereigns still cleared on borrower- and country-specific terms. CPI, PPI and retail sales are the immediate releases.
section_summaries.market_read: Near-term inflation relief improved risk tone without lowering the long-horizon cost of capital.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Real yields, auction levels and financing terms still constrain long-duration borrowers.
section_summaries.next_check: July 14 CPI starts the next inflation and curve repricing.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: The 2Y and 5Y led yields lower as crude and the 10Y breakeven fell, steepening 2s10s and 2s30s. Claims were stronger than expected and the 10Y real yield stayed at 2.31%, leaving lower inflation compensation as the rally's main channel. The 30Y reopening near 5.061% showed that long supply could clear, but only at a historically expensive level.
section_summaries.market_read: Breakeven relief richened the front end while real-rate and supply compensation held at the long end.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Nominal relief has not yet reduced the real financing hurdle.
section_summaries.next_check: CPI composition and the 30Y response around 5.06%.

section_summaries.summary: Listed credit edged higher and ION's 2032 bond gained 3.5 points on stronger earnings, but financing quality remained highly differentiated. Broadcom-linked private credit, TeraWulf leveraged debt, SK Hynix equity demand, MiniMax converts, Barclays acquisition loans and Shapoorji refinancing showed ample capital for defined structures across borrower tiers. Vestel's restructuring showed where market access still ended.
section_summaries.market_read: Funding channels remained open when cash flow, collateral, maturity relief or strategic demand supported the structure.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Execution terms determine whether better risk tone becomes cheaper corporate capital.
section_summaries.next_check: Order books, final pricing, retained exposure and secondary trading.

section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Japan's five-year auction found normal demand at 1.905%, although longer JGBs remained pressured by fiscal risk and a potentially higher BOJ terminal rate. Korea leaned toward another hike while Malaysia held at 2.75%; Bunds stabilized without erasing September ECB pricing. UK debt managers were already reconsidering £22.4 billion of planned long issuance as buyers demanded more compensation.
section_summaries.market_read: Global curves cleared through country-specific policy, fiscal and maturity concessions.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Competing sovereign supply helps keep long U.S. duration expensive.
section_summaries.next_check: Korea's auction and BOK decision, then UK GDP.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: A 0.13% decline in DXY and a 0.18% gain in EMB improved the common backdrop only modestly. Indonesia attracted foreign inflows through high carry and currency defense, China tightened weak LGFV refinancing while using most of its hidden-debt swap quota, and Senegal and Egypt remained tied to IMF access. Thailand and Peru retained policy room inside local fiscal and inflation limits.
section_summaries.market_read: Dollar pressure eased, but debt structure, program credibility and local inflation still controlled EM access.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: A gentler dollar cannot replace country-level balance-sheet repair.
section_summaries.next_check: Oil persistence, DXY and sovereign financing outcomes.

section_summaries.summary: Equities, semiconductors and lower volatility confirmed immediate risk relief as WTI and Brent fell more than 2%. The inflation signal remained fragile because fuel margins stayed elevated and Hormuz energy traffic nearly halted, separating futures from physical supply. A slightly softer dollar supported external conditions, while gold's 1.5% gain kept geopolitical and inflation hedging active.
section_summaries.market_read: Risk assets accepted lower nominal yields while physical energy and hedge signals remained unsettled.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Financial relief can reverse if product prices or geopolitical risk rebuild inflation pressure.
section_summaries.next_check: Crude, refined-product margins, Hormuz traffic and gold.

section_summaries.summary: The immediate agenda is dated and U.S.-led: CPI on July 14 sets the breakeven response, PPI on July 15 checks pipeline inflation, and retail sales on July 16 sets the demand and real-yield response. Korea's 10Y auction and BOK decision then price Asian duration, while UK GDP meets an unsettled gilt issuance plan. Distant PCE methodology changes stay behind these tradable events.
section_summaries.market_read: Next week's inflation and demand sequence will determine whether front-end relief reaches real yields and long duration.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The event order separates durable disinflation from a one-session energy adjustment.
section_summaries.next_check: CPI first, followed by PPI and retail sales.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Forward Market Significance carries the next market checks.
